text: Disability is a complex and multidimensional phenomenon that reflects the interaction between features of a person's body and the physical and social environment in which they live. It is not merely a medical condition or an individual deficit, but rather a dynamic interaction between health conditions and contextual factors – both personal and environmental. The World Health Organization (WHO) defines disability as: 'Disability is part of the human condition. Almost everyone will be temporarily or permanently impaired at some point in life, and those who survive to old age will experience increasing difficulties in functioning.'

text: In India, disability rights are protected under the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPWD Act). Key rights include equality, access to public spaces, education and employment, healthcare, and social security.

text: Blindness is a visual impairment in which a person has very low vision or no usable vision even after best correction. Under the RPWD Act 2016, blindness is recognized as a disability and may qualify for disability certification, assistive devices, education accommodations, and government benefits.

text: Locomotor disability refers to impairments that affect movement, muscle strength, or mobility. Under the RPWD Act 2016, locomotor disability is recognized as a benchmark disability and may qualify individuals for disability certification, assistive mobility devices, education accommodations, and government benefits.

text: Visual disabilities include blindness and low vision. In India, visual disability is covered under the RPWD Act 2016 and persons with visual impairment can access disability certification, assistive technology, education support, and reservations in employment and education.

text: Persons with disabilities can file complaints regarding discrimination or violation of rights with the Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities (CCPD) at the National level or State Commissioners at the State level. You can file a complaint online through the CCPD website (ccpdisabilities.nic.in) or by writing to the Commissioner. The Commissioners have powers of a civil court to hear grievances.

text: Under Section 32 of the RPWD Act 2016, all government and government-aided higher education institutions must reserve at least 5% seats for persons with benchmark disabilities. Furthermore, children with disabilities aged 6 to 18 years have the right to free education in a neighborhood school or a special school of their choice, as per Section 16 of the Act.

text: Under Section 34 of the RPWD Act, 2016, at least 4% reservation is provided in government establishments for persons with benchmark disabilities. text: The UDID (Unique Disability ID) card is a single document for identification and verification of persons with disabilities for availing various government benefits and schemes. It is valid across India and eliminates the need for multiple certificates for different purposes.

text: RPWD சட்டம், 2016 இன் பிரிவு 34ன் கீழ் அரசு அமைப்புகளில் benchmark மாற்றுத்திறனாளிகளுக்கு குறைந்தபட்சம் 4% இடஒதுக்கீடு வழங்கப்பட்டுள்ளது. இது மாற்றுத்திறனின் பல பிரிவுகளுக்கான அடையாளப்படுத்தப்பட்ட பணியிடங்களில் பொருந்தும். சாதாரண ஆட்சேர்ப்பு அறிவிப்புகள் மூலம் விண்ணப்பிக்கலாம்; RPWD விதிகளின்படி தளர்வுகள்/உதவிகளை பெறலாம்.

text: RPWD చట్టం, 2016 లోని సెక్షన్ 34 ప్రకారం ప్రభుత్వ సంస్థల్లో benchmark వికలాంగుల కోసం కనీసం 4% రిజర్వేషన్ ఉంది. ఇది వివిధ వికలాంగత వర్గాలకు గుర్తించిన పోస్టులకు వర్తిస్తుంది. సాధారణ నియామక ప్రకటనల ద్వారా దరఖాస్తు చేయవచ్చు మరియు RPWD నిబంధనల ప్రకారం వర్తించే సడలింపులు/సహాయం పొందవచ్చు.

text: RPWD ಕಾಯಿದೆ, 2016 ರ ಕಲಂ 34 ಅನ್ವಯ, ಸರ್ಕಾರದ ಸಂಸ್ಥೆಗಳಲ್ಲಿ benchmark ಅಂಗವಿಕಲ ವ್ಯಕ್ತಿಗಳಿಗೆ ಕನಿಷ್ಠ 4% ಮೀಸಲಾತಿ ನೀಡಲಾಗಿದೆ. ಇದು ವಿವಿಧ ಅಂಗವೈಕಲ್ಯ ವರ್ಗಗಳಿಗೆ ಗುರುತಿಸಲಾದ ಹುದ್ದೆಗಳಿಗೆ ಅನ್ವಯಿಸುತ್ತದೆ. ಸಾಮಾನ್ಯ ನೇಮಕಾತಿ ಅಧಿಸೂಚನೆಗಳ ಮೂಲಕ ಅರ್ಜಿ ಸಲ್ಲಿಸಿ, RPWD ನಿಯಮಗಳಡಿ ದೊರೆಯುವ ಸಡಿಲಿಕೆ/ಸಹಾಯವನ್ನು ಪಡೆಯಬಹುದು.

text: RPWD നിയമം, 2016 ലെ വകുപ്പ് 34 പ്രകാരം സർക്കാർ സ്ഥാപനങ്ങളിൽ benchmark വൈകല്യമുള്ള വ്യക്തികൾക്ക് കുറഞ്ഞത് 4% സംവരണം നൽകിയിട്ടുണ്ട്. ഇത് വിവിധ വൈകല്യ വിഭാഗങ്ങൾക്കുള്ള തിരിച്ചറിഞ്ഞ ഒഴിവുകൾക്ക് ബാധകമാണ്. സാധാരണ നിയമന വിജ്ഞാപനങ്ങൾ വഴി അപേക്ഷിക്കാം; RPWD ചട്ടങ്ങൾ പ്രകാരം ലഭ്യമായ ഇളവുകൾ/സഹായങ്ങൾ ആവശ്യപ്പെടാം.

text: ಸಂವೇದಿ ಅಂಗವಿಕಲತೆಗಳಲ್ಲಿ ದೃಷ್ಟಿ, ಕೇಳುವಿಕೆ, ಸ್ಪರ್ಶ, ರುಚಿ ಅಥವಾ ವಾಸನೆಯಲ್ಲಿರುವ ಅಶಕ್ತಿ ಸೇರಿವೆ. ಭಾರತದಲ್ಲಿ ಇವುಗಳನ್ನು RPWD ಕಾಯಿದೆಯಡಿ ಒಳಗೊಂಡಿರುತ್ತವೆ ಮತ್ತು ಪ್ರವೇಶ ಪ್ರಯೋಜನಗಳು, ಸಹಾಯಕ ಸಾಧನಗಳು, ಶಿಕ್ಷಣ ಸೌಲಭ್ಯಗಳು ಮತ್ತು ಅಂಗವೈಕಲತೆ ಪ್ರಮಾಣೀಕರಣಕ್ಕಾಗಿ ಅರ್ಹರಾಗಬಹುದು.

All statistics are sourced from official WHO, UN, and Government of India documents.

TABLE OF CONTENTS 1. Introduction to Disability 3 2. WHO – Global Disability Overview 5 3. WHO Key Statistics & Epidemiology 8 4. Types and Classifications of Disability (WHO/ICF) 11 5. CHAPTER 1: INTRODUCTION TO DISABILITY 1.1 Defining Disability Disability is a complex and multidimensional phenomenon that reflects the interaction between features of a person's body and the physical and social environment in which they live. It is not merely a medical condition or an individual deficit, but rather a dynamic interaction between health conditions and contextual factors – both personal and environmental. The understanding of disability has evolved significantly over the past few decades, shifting from a purely medical model to a biopsychosocial and human rights-based model. The World Health Organization (WHO), in its landmark World Report on Disability (2011), defines disability as follows: "Disability is part of the human condition. Almost everyone will be temporarily or permanently " "impaired at some point in life, and those who survive to old age will experience increasing " "difficulties in functioning. Most extended families have a disabled member, and many non-disabled " "people take responsibility for supporting and caring for their relatives and friends with disabilities." 1.2 Models of Disability Historically, disability has been understood through different conceptual models, each with its own implications for policy and practice:  Medical Model: Treats disability as a problem located within the individual, caused by disease, trauma, or other health conditions. The focus is on 'curing' or 'normalizing' the individual.  Social Model: Views disability as created by the social and physical environment that fails to accommodate people with impairments. Disability is a form of oppression caused by social barriers.  Biopsychosocial Model (WHO/ICF): Combines both medical and social perspectives. Disability arises from the interaction between an individual's health condition and contextual factors (environmental and personal). This is the basis of the International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF).  Human Rights Model: Views people with disabilities as rights holders. Disability is a matter of human rights, not charity. This model underpins the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD) and India's RPWD Act 2016. 1.3 The International Classification of Functioning (ICF)

The International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF), developed by WHO in 2001, represents the most comprehensive framework for understanding disability. It provides a standard language and framework for describing health and health-related states. The ICF classifies disability across three dimensions: ICF Component Definition Example Body Functions & Structures Physiological functions of body systems and anatomical parts Paralysis, visual impairment, hearing loss, limb absence Activities Execution of a task or action by an individual Difficulty walking, reading, or communicating Participation Involvement in a life situation Unable to attend school, work, or community events Environmental Factors Physical and social context that impact functioning Accessible buildings, policy support, social attitudes Personal Factors Individual background not part of health condition Age, gender, education, lifestyle choices 1.4 Why Disability Matters: A Global Imperative Disability is a global health and social issue of enormous magnitude. With approximately 1.3 billion people – roughly 16% of the world's population – living with some form of disability, it represents the world's largest minority group. People with disabilities experience disproportionate rates of poverty, social exclusion, and poor health outcomes. Despite this, disability often remains invisible in national data systems, policy frameworks, and development agendas. Addressing disability is therefore central to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and upholding universal human rights.

CHAPTER 2: WHO – GLOBAL DISABILITY OVERVIEW 2.1 WHO's Role in Disability Policy The World Health Organization plays a central role in shaping global disability policy, providing technical guidance, generating evidence, and supporting countries to build inclusive health systems. WHO's work on disability is organized under its Department of Noncommunicable Diseases, Sensory Functions, Disability and Rehabilitation. Key WHO milestones on disability include:  1976: First International Classification of Impairments, Disabilities and Handicaps (ICIDH) introduced.  2001: International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF) published.  2006: UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD) adopted; WHO was a key technical contributor.  2011: World Report on Disability – the first comprehensive global analysis of disability, published jointly by WHO and the World Bank.  2015: WHO Global Disability Action Plan 2014–2021 launched to improve the health of persons with disabilities.  2021: WHO Rehabilitation 2030 Initiative to scale up rehabilitation services globally.  2022: WHO Global Report on Health Equity for Persons with Disabilities published, the most recent major WHO flagship report. 2.2 The WHO World Report on Disability (2011) The World Report on Disability (WRD) was the first comprehensive global report on disability. It was jointly produced by WHO and the World Bank, drawing on evidence from over 100 countries. The report was launched at the United Nations headquarters in New York on 9 June 2011 and received widespread media and policy attention globally. The WRD examined disability across multiple domains, including healthcare, rehabilitation, assistance and support, enabling environments, education, employment, and social protection. It documented the circumstances of persons with disabilities across the world and explored measures to promote their social participation. Key Findings of the WRD (2011): ✓ Approximately 15% of the world's population – over 1 billion people – lives with disability. ✓ Between 110 million and 190 million adults experience significant difficulties in functioning.

✓ People with disabilities face significant barriers to accessing healthcare, education, and employment. ✓ Disability prevalence is higher in developing countries. ✓ Women with disabilities are particularly vulnerable to discrimination and violence. ✓ Children with disabilities are less likely to attend school or receive healthcare. ✓ People with disabilities experience higher rates of poverty than non-disabled persons. ✓ Majority of barriers faced by persons with disabilities are avoidable. 2.3 WHO Global Report on Health Equity for Persons with Disabilities (2022) In December 2022, WHO released its most recent flagship publication on disability – the Global Report on Health Equity for Persons with Disabilities. This report focused specifically on health disparities and called for urgent action to close the health gap between people with and without disabilities. The 2022 report highlighted that persons with disabilities:  Are twice as likely to have inadequate healthcare skills in their providers.  Are nearly three times more likely to be denied healthcare.  Are four times more likely to report being treated badly in healthcare settings.  Are more than twice as likely to find health-care providers' skills inadequate.  Experience 50% higher unmet healthcare needs compared to non-disabled persons.  Are more likely to experience catastrophic out-of-pocket health expenditures.  Face systematic exclusion from health promotion programs.

CHAPTER 3: WHO KEY STATISTICS & EPIDEMIOLOGY 3.1 Global Prevalence The scale of disability globally is staggering. According to the most recent WHO estimates (2023), approximately 1.3 billion people – or 16% of the global population – experience significant disability. This figure has grown significantly from WHO's earlier 1970s estimate of 10%, and even from the 2011 estimate of 15%. This growth is attributed to aging populations, the rise of chronic diseases, and improvements in measurement methodologies. Statistic Value Source/Year Global population with disability ~1.3 billion (16%) WHO, 2023 Adults with significant disability 110–190 million WHO, 2011 WRD People with vision impairment 2.2 billion WHO, 2019 People with hearing loss 1.5 billion (430M disabling) WHO, 2021 People with mental disorders 1 billion+ WHO, 2022 People needing rehab services 2.4 billion WHO, 2021 PwDs in low & middle income countries >80% of total PwDs WHO/World Bank Disability prevalence in older adults ~46% (aged 60+) WHO, 2011 Children with disabilities worldwide ~240 million (10%) UNICEF, 2021 PwDs living in poverty Disproportionately higher WHO/World Bank 3.2 Disability Prevalence by Region Disability prevalence varies significantly across regions, with low- and middle-income countries bearing a disproportionate burden due to poorer health infrastructure, higher rates of disease, injury, malnutrition, and inadequate access to rehabilitation services. Region Estimated Disability Prevalence Notes Sub-Saharan Africa 15–20% High infectious disease burden; limited data South Asia (incl. India) 15–20% Census data often underestimates East Asia & Pacific 10–15% Aging populations driving increase Latin America & Caribbean 12–18% Road traffic injury a major factor High-income countries 10–15% More comprehensive data systems Globally (overall) ~16% WHO 2023 estimate 3.3 Disability and Age

Disability is strongly correlated with age. The WHO estimates that approximately 46% of people aged 60 years and older have some form of disability, compared to about 15% of the general population. This pattern has major implications for healthcare systems as global populations age rapidly, particularly in low- and middle-income countries. 3.4 Disability and Gender Disability disproportionately affects women. Women with disabilities face double discrimination – first as women in patriarchal societies, and second as persons with disabilities in an inaccessible world. Key gender-related disability statistics include:  Women with disabilities are more likely to experience gender-based violence than non-disabled women.  Women with disabilities have higher rates of unmet healthcare needs, including reproductive health.  In many countries, girls with disabilities are less likely to attend school.  In India, female PwDs face compounded disadvantage in employment and marriage.  WHO data shows women face unique barriers in rehabilitation service access.

CHAPTER 4: TYPES AND CLASSIFICATIONS OF DISABILITY (WHO/ICF) The WHO, through the International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF), provides a comprehensive taxonomy of disability categories. These classifications are used globally by health systems, policy makers, and researchers. 4.1 Physical / Locomotor Disabilities Physical disabilities affect mobility, motor functions, and the overall ability to move and perform physical tasks. They may be congenital or acquired through illness, injury, or aging. Locomotor Disability Impairment of limbs or musculoskeletal system affecting movement. Includes conditions such as paraplegia, quadriplegia, polio, cerebral palsy, clubfoot, and amputation. WHO estimates over 450 million people have musculoskeletal conditions globally. Cerebral Palsy A group of non-progressive neurological disorders affecting movement, muscle tone, and motor skills. Caused by damage to the brain before, during, or shortly after birth. Prevalence: 2–3 per 1,000 live births globally. Muscular Dystrophy A group of genetic diseases causing progressive muscle weakness and degeneration. Over 30 types are recognized. Duchenne MD is the most common, affecting approximately 1 in 3,500 male births. Dwarfism Characterized by short stature resulting from genetic or medical conditions. Achondroplasia is the most common cause, occurring in approximately 1 in 25,000 births. Acid Attack Injuries Permanent physical disability resulting from intentional acid attacks. Disfiguration, vision loss, and mobility impairments are common consequences. 4.2 Sensory Disabilities Blindness Complete loss of vision. Globally, approximately 43 million people are blind (WHO, 2019). Leading causes include cataract (51%), glaucoma (8%), age-related macular degeneration (5%), corneal opacity (4%), and diabetic retinopathy (1%). About 80% of global blindness is avoidable.

Low Vision Visual impairment not correctable to normal vision with standard glasses. Approximately 2.2 billion people have near or distance vision impairment, of whom at least 1 billion have impairment that could have been prevented. Low vision includes tunnel vision, central vision loss, and contrast sensitivity impairment. Hearing Impairment (Deaf) Profound hearing loss of 70dB or more in the better ear. WHO (2021) estimates 1.5 billion people have some hearing loss, with 430 million having disabling hearing loss. By 2050, over 700 million people may experience disabling hearing loss. Key causes: noise exposure, infections, aging. Hard of Hearing Moderate to severe hearing loss (60–70 dB). These individuals can communicate through spoken language with or without hearing aids. Early detection and intervention are critical to language development in children. Speech and Language Disability Impairment in communication, language, or speech production. Includes stuttering, aphasia, dysarthria, and voice disorders. Approximately 5% of children have a speech or language disorder by age 8. 4.3 Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities Intellectual Disability Characterized by significant limitations in both intellectual functioning (reasoning, learning, problem solving) and adaptive behavior. IQ below 70. Global prevalence estimated at 1–3%. Previously termed 'mental retardation'; now replaced with 'intellectual disability' in most legislation including RPWD Act 2016. Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) A developmental disorder affecting social interaction, communication, and behavior. WHO estimates 1 in 100 children globally has autism. Recent US CDC data suggests 1 in 36 children. Male:female ratio is approximately 4:1. Specific Learning Disabilities (SLD) A heterogeneous group of disorders including dyslexia (reading), dyscalculia (mathematics), and dysgraphia (writing). Affect 5–15% of school-age children globally. Not related to intellectual ability. Down Syndrome A chromosomal condition caused by trisomy 21. Associated with intellectual disability and physical features. Prevalence: approximately 1 in 1,000 live births globally (higher with

4.4 Mental Health Disabilities WHO recognizes mental disorders as significant causes of disability. The Global Burden of Disease study consistently ranks mental disorders among the leading causes of disability worldwide. Depression is the single largest cause of disability globally. Mental Illness Includes substantial disorders of thinking, mood, perception, and behavior that impair judgment and daily functioning. WHO (2022) estimates 1 in 8 people globally lives with a mental disorder. Major types include depression, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, and anxiety disorders. Depression Affects 280 million people globally (WHO, 2021). Leading cause of disability worldwide. Characterized by persistent sadness, loss of interest, sleep disturbance, and impaired concentration. Treatable but grossly undertreated, especially in LMICs. Schizophrenia Affects approximately 24 million people globally (WHO). Characterized by hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized thinking. Significantly impacts social and occupational functioning. Major cause of psychiatric disability. Bipolar Disorder Affects approximately 45 million people globally. Characterized by alternating episodes of mania and depression. Can cause severe disruption to work and relationships. Anxiety Disorders Most prevalent mental disorders globally. Affect approximately 301 million people worldwide (WHO, 2019). Include generalized anxiety disorder, panic disorder, social anxiety, PTSD, and OCD. 4.5 Neurological Disabilities Multiple Sclerosis (MS) A chronic, inflammatory, demyelinating disease of the central nervous system. Affects approximately 2.8 million people worldwide. Can cause progressive disability including mobility problems, visual disturbance, fatigue, and cognitive changes. Parkinson's Disease A progressive neurological disorder affecting movement, with tremors, rigidity, and bradykinesia. Affects over 8.5 million people globally (WHO, 2022). Second most common neurodegenerative disease after Alzheimer's. Epilepsy

Characterized by recurrent seizures. Approximately 50 million people have epilepsy globally (WHO). Around 80% live in low- and middle-income countries. A significant cause of disability and social stigma. Chronic Neurological Conditions Includes a range of conditions such as motor neurone disease (ALS), Huntington's disease, and other neurodegenerative disorders causing progressive physical and cognitive disability. 4.6 Blood Disorders as Disabilities India's RPWD Act 2016 uniquely includes three blood disorders as recognized disabilities, reflecting the high burden of these conditions in the Indian population: Haemophilia An inherited bleeding disorder in which blood doesn't clot properly due to deficiency of clotting factors (VIII or IX). Affects approximately 1 in 10,000 births globally. Can cause spontaneous bleeding, joint damage, and chronic disability. Thalassemia A group of inherited blood disorders causing abnormal hemoglobin production. Over 300,000 affected births annually worldwide, with India having the highest burden. Severe forms cause chronic anemia, organ damage, and developmental disability. Sickle Cell Disease An inherited disorder affecting the shape of red blood cells, causing pain crises, anemia, and organ damage. Affects over 20 million people globally, with significant burden in tribal populations across India.

CHAPTER 5: WHO – HEALTH INEQUITIES & BARRIERS FACED BY PwDs 5.1 Healthcare Access According to WHO, persons with disabilities face significant and systematic barriers to accessing health services. These barriers manifest across multiple levels – from physical inaccessibility of healthcare facilities to discriminatory attitudes of healthcare providers. Barrier/Disparity Data Point Comparison Unable to afford healthcare 50% of PwDs 33% of non-disabled Denied healthcare Nearly 3x more likely vs. non-disabled persons Treated badly in healthcare 4x more likely vs. non-disabled persons Inadequate provider skills >2x more likely to report vs. non-disabled persons Catastrophic health expenditure Significantly higher WHO 2022 Report Unmet rehabilitation needs >50% globally WHO Rehab 2030 Mental health service gap Treatment gap >70% In low-income countries 5.2 Types of Barriers (WHO Classification)  Physical/Environmental Barriers: Inaccessible buildings, transport, information, and communication. Lack of ramps, lifts, accessible toilets, and tactile pathways.  Attitudinal Barriers: Negative stereotypes, stigma, discrimination, and low expectations from family, community, employers, and healthcare providers.  Institutional Barriers: Policies, procedures, and organizational structures that fail to accommodate persons with disabilities. Absence of reasonable accommodation.  Communication Barriers: Lack of sign language interpreters, Braille materials, easy-read information, and accessible digital content.  Financial Barriers: High cost of assistive devices, healthcare, and transport. Low income due to employment exclusion creates a 'disability poverty trap.'  Information Barriers: Lack of awareness among PwDs about their rights, available services, and how to navigate health and social systems. 5.3 Disability and Poverty – The Vicious Cycle WHO and World Bank data consistently show that disability and poverty are closely linked in a self-reinforcing cycle. Disability can cause poverty through reduced employment, increased medical costs, reduced productivity, and social exclusion. Conversely, poverty can cause disability through poor nutrition, exposure to disease, unsafe living conditions, and limited access to healthcare.

Key findings on disability and poverty: → In OECD countries, the employment rate of PwDs (44%) is barely half that of non-disabled (75%). → People with disabilities earn 30–40% less than non-disabled workers in many countries. → Households with a disabled member spend more on healthcare and support. → In India, 69% of PwDs reside in rural areas with minimal service access. → The 'extra cost of disability' (assistive devices, caregiving, transport) deepens poverty.

CHAPTER 6: WHO WORLD REPORT ON DISABILITY – KEY CHAPTER FINDINGS 6.1 Rehabilitation Services Rehabilitation is defined by WHO as 'a set of interventions designed to optimize functioning and reduce disability in individuals with health conditions in interaction with their environment.' Despite its critical importance, rehabilitation remains severely underfunded and underutilized globally.  An estimated 2.4 billion people currently need rehabilitation services globally (WHO, 2021).  Only 1 in 3 people who need rehabilitation access it globally.  In low-income countries, the gap is far worse – over 90% of needs remain unmet.  Rehabilitation reduces hospitalization duration by 25–30% and improves functional outcomes.  Key rehabilitation services include physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, prosthetics and orthotics, and psychosocial rehabilitation. 6.2 Assistive Technology Assistive technology (AT) enables persons with disabilities to live independent, productive lives. WHO estimates that over 1 billion people need assistive technology, but fewer than 10% have access globally. This gap is particularly severe in low-income countries. Assistive Device Estimated Need Current Access Hearing Aids 466 million people ~17% have access Wheelchairs 70+ million people <5% in LMICs White canes/visual aids 253 million (vision impaired) Low access in LMICs Communication devices (AAC) Millions with comm. disability Very limited globally Prosthetics/Orthotics 30+ million amputees 3–10% in LMICs 6.3 Education Education is a fundamental right and a key determinant of life outcomes. However, children with disabilities face significant barriers to education, despite progress in inclusive education policies worldwide.  Children with disabilities are less likely to attend school than non-disabled children in every country studied by WHO.  In Bolivia, 98% of non-disabled children attend school but under 40% of disabled children do.

 In Indonesia, over 80% of non-disabled children attend school, but less than 25% of disabled children do.  Education completion gaps persist across all age groups, with the pattern more pronounced in poorer countries.  Globally, there are approximately 240 million children with disabilities; most lack adequate educational support.  Lack of trained teachers, accessible schools, and adapted curricula are the main barriers. 6.4 Employment Employment is critical for economic independence and social inclusion. However, persons with disabilities face systematic exclusion from the labor market, both in formal and informal sectors.  In OECD countries, employment rate of PwDs is 44% vs 75% for non-disabled persons.  In developing countries, the employment gap is wider, with many PwDs engaged only in subsistence activities.  Persons with intellectual, psychosocial, or multiple disabilities face the greatest employment barriers.  Workplace discrimination, inaccessible environments, and employer prejudice are major barriers.  Supported employment models have shown significant success in integrating PwDs into competitive employment.

CHAPTER 7: WHO RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTION FRAMEWORK 7.1 WHO Global Disability Action Plan 2014–2021 The WHO Global Disability Action Plan (GDAP) 2014–2021 provided a framework for countries to improve health and well-being for persons with disabilities. It was built on three intersecting objectives:  Objective 1: Remove barriers and improve access to health services and programmes.  Objective 2: Strengthen and extend rehabilitation, habilitation, assistive technology, assistance and support services, and community-based rehabilitation.  Objective 3: Strengthen collection of relevant and internationally comparable data on disability and support research on disability and related services. 7.2 Nine Cross-Cutting WHO Recommendations The World Report on Disability (2011) highlighted nine cross-cutting recommendations applicable to all sectors and governments: 1. Enable access to all mainstream systems and services. 2. Invest in specific programmes and services for people with disabilities. 3. Adopt a national disability strategy and plan of action. 4. Involve people with disabilities. 5. Improve human resource capacity. 6. Provide adequate funding and improve affordability. 7. Increase public awareness and understanding of disability. 8. Improve data collection on disability. 9. Strengthen and support research on disability. 7.3 WHO Rehabilitation 2030 In 2017, WHO launched the Rehabilitation 2030 initiative to address the growing global unmet need for rehabilitation. The initiative calls for strong national rehabilitation systems and sustainable rehabilitation service delivery integrated into health systems. Key strategies include: → Strengthening leadership and governance for rehabilitation at national level. → Developing a sufficient, well-trained, and equitably distributed rehabilitation workforce. → Ensuring financing for rehabilitation through health insurance and social protection.

→ Integrating rehabilitation into all levels of health systems (primary, secondary, tertiary). → Implementing community-based rehabilitation (CBR) approaches. → Expanding access to quality assistive products in countries.

CHAPTER 8: INDIA'S DISABILITY LANDSCAPE – CENSUS & DATA 8.1 Disability Prevalence in India India is home to one of the largest populations of persons with disabilities in the world. According to the Census of India 2011 – the most recent national census with disability data – approximately 26.8 million people (2.21% of the total population) reported having some form of disability. However, this figure is widely acknowledged to be a significant underestimate, due to definitional limitations, stigma, lack of awareness, and non-inclusion of many disability types. Note: WHO estimates that disability prevalence in India could be as high as 10–20%, consistent with global norms. The significant gap between the Census 2011 figure (2.21%) and WHO estimates is a subject of ongoing academic and policy debate. Disability Type Number (Census 2011) % of PwD Population In Seeing (Vision) 50,30,828 18.8% In Hearing 50,70,914 18.9% In Speech 19,98,341 7.5% In Movement (Locomotor) 54,36,459 20.3% Mental Retardation (Intellectual) 15,05,624 5.6% Mental Illness 7,22,826 2.7% Multiple Disability 22,26,075 8.3% Any Other Disability 47,85,807 17.9% TOTAL 2,68,10,557 100% 8.2 Demographic Characteristics of PwDs in India Characteristic Data/Statistic Gender distribution Male: 56% / Female: 44% Rural vs Urban Rural: 69.4% / Urban: 30.6% Literacy rate (PwDs) 54.5% (vs. national 74%) Main worker rate (PwDs) 34.4% (vs. 39.8% general) Children with disability (0–14 yrs) ~5.3 million PwDs receiving disability pension Very low coverage (under 20%) 8.3 Disability in India – Key Policy Context India's disability policy landscape has evolved significantly over the past three decades. The key milestones include:

 1992: National Policy for Persons with Disabilities drafted.  1995: Persons with Disabilities (Equal Opportunities, Protection of Rights and Full Participation) Act enacted. Recognized 7 types of disability.  1999: National Trust Act – for persons with autism, cerebral palsy, intellectual disability, and multiple disabilities.  2007: India ratified the UN Convention on Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD).  2012: National Policy on Disability finalized.  2015: Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan) launched by Government of India.  2016: Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act (RPWD Act) enacted, replacing 1995 Act. Expanded to 21 disability categories.  2017: RPWD Act came into effect (April 19, 2017).

CHAPTER 9: RPWD ACT 2016 – OVERVIEW AND BACKGROUND 9.1 Introduction to the RPWD Act 2016 The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPWD Act / RPwD Act) is a landmark piece of legislation enacted by the Indian Parliament on December 16, 2016. It received Presidential assent on December 27, 2016, was notified in the Gazette of India on December 28, 2016, and came into force on April 19, 2017. The Act is also officially referred to as 'Act 49 of 2016.' The RPWD Act replaces the earlier Persons with Disabilities (Equal Opportunities, Protection of Rights and Full Participation) Act of 1995 (PwD Act, 1995), which was considered inadequate and out of step with contemporary understandings of disability rights. 9.2 Purpose and Objective The primary objective of the RPWD Act 2016 is to give effect to the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD, 2006), which India signed on March 30, 2007, and ratified on October 1, 2007. The Act aims to: ✓ Ensure that every person with a disability can live their life with dignity, without discrimination, and with equal opportunity. ✓ Promote, protect, and ensure the full and equal enjoyment of all human rights by persons with disabilities. ✓ Promote respect for their inherent dignity. ✓ Ensure non-discrimination on the basis of disability. ✓ Enable full and effective participation in all spheres of society. ✓ Provide for reasonable accommodation in education and employment. 9.3 Structure of the Act The RPWD Act 2016 is a comprehensive legislation consisting of 102 sections organized across 17 chapters, plus a Schedule listing the 21 specified disabilities. Chapter Title Key Sections Chapter 1 Preliminary – Definitions Sec. 1–2 (Definitions) Chapter 2 Rights & Entitlements Sec. 3–23 (Non-discrimination, healthcare, education, etc.) Chapter 3 Duties of Gov. Establishments Sec. 20–22 (Employment, equal opportunity) Chapter 4 Education Sec. 16–18 (Inclusive education) Chapter 5 Employment Sec. 19–24 (Reservation, grievance)

Chapter 6 Social Security, Health, Rehab Sec. 24–30 (Schemes, insurance) Chapter 7 Community & Recreational Life Sec. 30 (Access to culture, sports) Chapter 8 Legal Capacity & Guardianship Sec. 14–15 (District Court guardianship) Chapter 9–11 Registration, Certification Unique Disability ID (UDID) Chapter 12–13 Regulatory & Advisory Bodies Chief Commissioner, Advisory Boards Chapter 14–15 National & State Fund Sec. 86–90 (Financial provisions) Chapter 16 Offences & Penalties Sec. 91–95 (Criminal provisions) Chapter 17 Miscellaneous Sec. 96–102

The Central Government is also empowered to add more types by notification. 10.3 Detailed Profiles of Each Disability Under RPWD Act 1. BLINDNESS Definition under RPWD Act: A condition where a person has any of the following conditions: (a) total absence of sight; (b) visual acuity not exceeding 3/60 or 10/200 (Snellen) in the better eye with best possible spectacle correction; (c) limitation of field of vision subtending an angle of less than 10 degrees. Causes: Cataract (most common in India), glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy, trachoma, retinitis pigmentosa, corneal opacity, vitamin A deficiency. India Data: Over 12 million persons are blind in India. India accounts for nearly 20% of the global blind population. National Programme for Control of Blindness (NPCB) is the key government initiative. 2. LOW VISION Definition: A condition where a person has any of the following conditions after best correction: (a) visual acuity not exceeding 6/18 or less than 10/60 up to 3/60; (b) limitation of field of vision between 10 and 40 degrees. Low vision requires specialized rehabilitation including optical devices (magnifiers, telescopes), non-optical aids, and environmental modifications. Unlike blindness, low vision can often be functionally improved with proper aids. 3. LEPROSY CURED Leprosy (Hansen's Disease) is caused by Mycobacterium leprae. Despite being cured medically, many individuals retain permanent disabilities including clawing of fingers, absorption of digits, foot drop, lagophthalmos (inability to close eyes), and skin changes. India Context: India accounts for approximately 60% of global new leprosy cases annually. The RPWD Act explicitly protects persons cured of leprosy from discrimination, a significant step as they face severe social stigma. 4 & 5. HEARING IMPAIRMENT Hearing Impairment in RPWD Act covers two sub-categories:  Deaf: 70 dB or more hearing loss in the better ear in conversational range of frequencies.  Hard of Hearing: 60–70 dB hearing loss in the better ear. Causes: Congenital (maternal rubella, birth asphyxia), infections (meningitis, chronic ear infections), noise-induced, ototoxic drugs, aging. India Data: ~50.7 lakh persons with hearing impairment (Census 2011). National Programme for Prevention and Control of Deafness (NPPCD) provides free hearing aids.

6. SPEECH AND LANGUAGE DISABILITY New in RPWD 2016. Defined as a permanent disability arising out of conditions such as laryngectomy or aphasia affecting one or more components of speech and language due to organic or neurological causes. Types: Aphasia (language disorder after brain injury), dysarthria (motor speech disorder), stuttering, voice disorders (dysphonia, laryngectomy). This was added recognizing that inability to communicate effectively is a significant disability impacting education, employment, and social participation. 7. LOCOMOTOR DISABILITY Defined as a person's inability to execute distinctive activities associated with moving oneself and objects resulting from affliction of musculoskeletal or nervous system. India Census 2011: Largest disability group – ~54.36 lakh persons (20.3% of all PwDs). Causes: Polio (historically major in India; now near-elimination post-eradication), paralysis, stroke, spinal cord injury, accidents, congenital conditions, arthritis/bone disorders. This category also includes partial or complete loss of limbs (amputation). 8. DWARFISM Defined as a medical or genetic condition resulting in an adult height of 4 feet 10 inches (147 cm) or less. More than 200 medical conditions can cause dwarfism. Achondroplasia is the most common (approximately 70% of cases). Average height of a person with achondroplasia is 4 feet (122 cm). The inclusion recognizes unique mobility, healthcare, and social challenges. 9. INTELLECTUAL DISABILITY Defined as a condition characterized by significant limitation in both intellectual functions (reasoning, learning, problem solving) and adaptive behavior (conceptual, social and practical skills) that originate before the age of 18 years. Types under RPWD Act: (a) Specific intellectual disability; (b) Autism Spectrum Disorder. Causes: Genetic (Down syndrome, Fragile X), prenatal infections, birth complications, severe malnutrition, lead poisoning, meningitis. India Data: ~15 lakh persons (Census 2011), significant underestimate. 10. SPECIFIC LEARNING DISABILITIES (SLD)

New in RPWD 2016. A heterogeneous group of conditions wherein there is a deficit in processing language, spoken or written, that may manifest itself as a difficulty to comprehend, speak, read, write, spell, or to do mathematical calculations and includes such conditions as perceptual disabilities, dyslexia, dyscalculia, dysgraphia, dyspraxia, and developmental aphasia. Key Types: Dyslexia (reading/writing), Dyscalculia (math), Dysgraphia (writing), Dyspraxia (movement coordination). India: Estimated 10–15% of school children may have some form of SLD. 11. AUTISM SPECTRUM DISORDER (ASD) A neuro-developmental condition characterized by challenges with social interaction, communication, and restricted/repetitive behaviors. Spectrum ranges from mildly affected to severely disabled. RPWD Act recognizes ASD as a distinct disability eligible for all protections including reservation in education and employment. This was a significant advance over the 1995 Act. 12. MENTAL ILLNESS Defined as a substantial disorder of thinking, mood, perception, orientation, or memory that grossly impairs judgment, behavior, capacity to recognize reality or ability to meet the ordinary demands of life, but does not include retardation which is a condition of arrested or incomplete development of mind. Key conditions: Schizophrenia, major depression, bipolar disorder, severe anxiety disorders, OCD, PTSD, personality disorders. India: ~150 million people need active mental health interventions (NMHS 2015–16). Only 3–4 psychiatrists per million population (WHO norm: 10+). 13. CEREBRAL PALSY A group of permanent disorders of the development of movement and posture, causing activity limitations attributed to non-progressive disturbances in the developing fetal or infant brain. Types: Spastic (most common), Athetoid/Dyskinetic, Ataxic, Mixed. India: Prevalence ~3 per 1,000 live births. Approximately 25 lakh children in India have cerebral palsy. Closely linked to birth asphyxia (hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy). 18. HAEMOPHILIA

An inherited bleeding disorder caused by deficiency of clotting factor VIII (Haemophilia A) or factor IX (Haemophilia B). Causes spontaneous or trauma-induced bleeding into joints and muscles, leading to chronic joint disease and locomotor disability. India: ~1 in 10,000 males affected. The Haemophilia Federation of India estimates ~1.5 lakh individuals with haemophilia in India, though many are undiagnosed. 19. THALASSEMIA An inherited hemoglobin disorder caused by mutations affecting globin chain production. Beta-thalassemia major requires lifelong blood transfusions causing chronic disability. India Context: India has the highest number of thalassemia major patients globally (~1 lakh children). 10,000–15,000 children with thalassemia major born in India annually. An estimated 4.4 crore Indians are carriers (thalassemia trait/minor). 20. SICKLE CELL DISEASE An inherited hemoglobin disorder where red blood cells become sickle-shaped, causing vaso-occlusive crises, severe pain, chronic anemia, and organ damage. India: High prevalence in tribal populations of Central India (Maharashtra, MP, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Gujarat). National Mission on Sickle Cell Anaemia launched 2023. Estimated 20 million carriers and 2 lakh patients in India. 21. ACID ATTACK VICTIMS Persons who are disfigured due to violent assaults by throwing of acid on their faces or body. The RPWD Act 2016 uniquely includes acid attack victims recognizing the permanent physical, sensory (vision loss, hearing loss), and psychological disabilities they acquire. India Context: India has one of the highest rates of acid attacks globally. 300+ cases reported annually (National Crime Records Bureau), with significant underreporting. Supreme Court has directed stricter regulation of acid sale.

CHAPTER 11: RPWD ACT 2016 – RIGHTS, ENTITLEMENTS & PROVISIONS 11.1 Fundamental Rights Provided  Right to Equality and Non-Discrimination (Sec. 3): No person with disability shall be discriminated on ground of disability unless the act is a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim. Women and children with disabilities shall have equal rights.  Right to Life with Dignity: Appropriate Government shall ensure PwDs enjoy right to equality, life with dignity and respect for integrity equally with others.  Protection from Cruelty and Inhuman Treatment: No PwD shall be subject to torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment. Protection from abuse, violence and exploitation.  Right to Community Inclusion: Appropriate Government to take steps to enable PwDs to live in the community. This includes access to housing, social services, and support.  Reproductive Rights: PwDs have right to decide freely the number, spacing, and timing of children. Right to retain fertility. This was a landmark recognition in India.  Right to Legal Capacity: PwDs have legal capacity on equal basis with others in all aspects of life (Sec. 14). District Court may appoint limited guardian only as last resort.  Right to Vote: Election Commission to ensure all polling stations are accessible. Facilities for voting to be provided including assistance.  Right to Own Property: PwDs have right to equal ownership of property and right to inherit movable and immovable property. 11.2 Education – Key Provisions (Sec. 16–18) → Right to free and compulsory education for children with disabilities in neighborhood school. → Special schools to be established for those who cannot be accommodated in mainstream. → Reservation of seats in higher educational institutions (minimum 5% seats). → Scholarship to students with benchmark disabilities in higher education. → Appointment of 'Special Educators' in schools. → Accommodation in examinations – extra time, scribe, assistive devices. → Barrier-free accessible school buildings mandated. → Home-based education option for those unable to attend school.

→ Recognition and promotion of sign language; development of curriculum in sign language. 11.3 Employment – Key Provisions (Sec. 19–24) Provision Details Reservation in Government Jobs Minimum 4% of total vacancies reserved for PwDs (increased from 3% under 1995 Act) Benchmark Disability Threshold of 40% disability for eligibility for reservations Equal Opportunity Policy Every employer to prepare & notify Equal Opportunity Policy Grievance Redressal Appointment of Liaison Officer by all establishments Training Programs Vocational training and self-employment schemes Private Sector Incentives Government to provide incentives to private employers hiring PwDs UDID Requirement Unique Disability ID required for job reservation benefits 11.4 Social Security and Healthcare  PwDs with benchmark disability to be given insurance scheme (Sec. 24).  Rehabilitation Council of India to maintain minimum standards in rehabilitation services.  Early intervention services for children with disabilities.  Comprehensive rehabilitation services in government hospitals.  Reproductive healthcare and birth spacing for women with disabilities.  National and State Funds for PwD welfare and financial assistance.  Indira Gandhi National Disability Pension Scheme for below poverty line PwDs. 11.5 Accessibility Provisions (Sec. 40–46) The RPWD Act places strong emphasis on accessibility as a prerequisite for inclusion. These provisions align with the Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan) launched by the Government of India in 2015. → Physical accessibility standards for all government buildings within prescribed timeframe. → Accessible roads, transport, and infrastructure. → Accessible information and communication including websites, documents, and signage. → All public and private service providers must comply with accessibility standards. → Access to judiciary – accessible courts and legal processes. → Development and implementation of Standards for Accessibility.

CHAPTER 12: RPWD ACT 2016 – IMPLEMENTATION & CHALLENGES 12.1 Institutional Mechanisms The RPWD Act establishes a multi-level institutional framework for implementation:  Chief Commissioner for PwDs (National): Regulatory and grievance redressal body. Monitors implementation; hears complaints from PwDs. To be supported by 2 Commissioners and an Advisory Committee.  State Commissioners for PwDs: Equivalent body at the state level. Monitor state implementation, address local grievances.  District Level Committees: Address local concerns; constituted by State Governments.  Central Advisory Board on Disability: Apex policy body with representatives from ministries, experts, disabled persons' organizations.  State Advisory Boards: Equivalent bodies at state level for policy guidance.  National and State Funds: Financial mechanisms to support PwD welfare programs. Existing funds subsumed under the new National Fund. 12.2 Unique Disability ID (UDID) A major implementation initiative is the Unique Disability ID (UDID) card system, a national database of persons with disabilities. UDID provides:  Standardized disability certificate across the country.  Digital database enabling tracking of benefits and services.  Technology-enabled monitoring of implementation.  Prevention of duplicate certificates and fraudulent claims.  Smart card with embedded chip containing disability information. 12.3 Challenges and Gaps in Implementation Despite the progressive provisions of the RPWD Act 2016, implementation has faced numerous challenges. The Supreme Court of India, in Seema Girija Lal & Anr Vs Union of India & Ors (2022–2023), expressed strong disappointment at inadequate implementation across states including Andhra Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Uttar Pradesh, Punjab, Tripura, and UT of Chandigarh. ■ Data Deficit: Census 2011 (the most recent national disability data) significantly underestimates disability prevalence. New census and specialized disability survey needed.

■ Rural-Urban Gap: 69% of PwDs reside in rural areas where services, assistive devices, rehabilitation centers, and awareness are severely lacking. ■ Inadequate Funding: SIPDA (Scheme for Implementation of Persons with Disabilities Act) budget increased by less than 9% over five years (2016–2021) despite growing need. ■ Pension Inadequacy: Indira Gandhi National Disability Pension of Rs. 300–500/month is grossly insufficient given inflation. ■ Limited Private Sector Compliance: Reservations in private sector employment are voluntary. Most private employers do not implement accessibility or equal opportunity policies. ■ State Inaction: Many states have not constituted District-Level Committees, Advisory Boards, or State Funds as mandated by the Act. ■ Social Audit Gap: Section 48 mandating social audit of disability schemes has not been implemented on priority basis. ■ Coordination Failure: Non-receipt of Utilization Certificates from states results in non-release of central funds for disability initiatives. ■ Awareness Deficit: Majority of PwDs and their families are unaware of their rights, entitlements, and available schemes under RPWD Act 2016.

CHAPTER 13: DISABILITY AND EDUCATION IN INDIA 13.1 Education Policy and Disability Education is both a fundamental right (Article 21A of the Constitution) and a key pathway to social inclusion for persons with disabilities. India's disability-education landscape is governed by multiple policy frameworks including the RPWD Act 2016, Right to Education Act 2009, National Education Policy 2020 (NEP 2020), and Samagra Shiksha scheme. 13.2 Key Education Statistics for PwDs in India Indicator Statistic Comparison (General) Literacy rate of PwDs 54.5% National: 74.04% School enrollment (disabled children) Low – significant out-of-school population RTE guarantees universal enrollment Dropout rate Higher than national average Particularly after Class 5 Access to mainstream school Improving but barriers remain Majority prefer integrated education Higher education enrollment of PwDs Less than 1% of total enrollment 5% reservation mandated Special schools (NIOS, State run) Limited in number and geographic reach Urban concentration 13.3 National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 and Disability The National Education Policy 2020 emphasizes inclusive education for children with disabilities. Key provisions include:  Foundational literacy and numeracy programs adapted for all learners including PwDs.  National Curriculum Framework to include disability-inclusive content.  Teacher education programs to include compulsory module on inclusive education.  Universal Design for Learning (UDL) to be adopted across educational institutions.  NIOS (National Institute of Open Schooling) to be strengthened for PwDs.  Sign Language as a language of learning to be promoted.  Assistive technology in education to be expanded. 13.4 Samagra Shiksha and Inclusive Education Samagra Shiksha (2018) is the flagship integrated school education scheme that includes a strong inclusive education component. Under Samagra Shiksha: → Assessment camps and Individualized Education Plans (IEPs) for children with disabilities. → Resource rooms and home-based education programs.

→ Therapeutic and corrective equipment and aids to children with disabilities. → Orientation and mobility training; Braille kits for visually impaired. → Appointment of special educators in cluster resource centers. → Teacher sensitization and training on inclusive education.

CHAPTER 14: DISABILITY AND EMPLOYMENT – INDIA & GLOBAL 14.1 Global Employment Scenario Employment is critical for economic independence, self-worth, and social inclusion of persons with disabilities. However, globally, PwDs face significant labor market exclusion. Employment Indicator PwDs Non-disabled Employment rate (OECD avg.) 44% 75% Part-time employment Disproportionately higher Lower Average earnings 30–40% lower Baseline Working poverty rate Higher Lower Informal employment Much higher in LMICs Lower proportion 14.2 Employment in India – RPWD Act Provisions and Reality The RPWD Act 2016 mandates 4% reservation in government jobs for PwDs (up from 3% under 1995 Act). Despite this legal provision, actual employment of PwDs in government establishments remains far below targets.  Only 34.4% of PwDs are 'main workers' vs 39.8% in general population (Census 2011).  Employment opportunities in rural areas for PwDs are predominantly limited to agriculture.  Government has introduced National Career Service Centres (NCSCs) for PwDs.  Vocational rehabilitation centers (VRCs) established under MSJE to provide training.  NHFDC (National Handicapped Finance and Development Corporation) provides loans for self-employment.  Scheme for Rehabilitation of Persons with Disability (under DDRS) provides training.  Corporate sector has begun to hire PwDs under ESG mandates – still early-stage.  Mental illness and intellectual disability face the highest barriers to employment. 14.3 Supported Employment and Best Practices International evidence strongly supports the 'supported employment' model as the most effective approach to integrating PwDs – especially those with intellectual disabilities and psychosocial conditions – into competitive employment.  Job Matching: Careful assessment of individual's skills and interests matched to appropriate jobs.  Job Coach Support: Intensive on-site job coaching during initial employment period.

 Employer Sensitization: Training and support for employers to create inclusive workplaces.  Natural Supports: Building peer support networks in workplace.  Follow-Along Support: Long-term, as-needed support after initial placement.

CHAPTER 15: CONCLUSION AND RESEARCH GAPS 15.1 Summary of Key Findings This comprehensive reference document has synthesized data from WHO global publications and India's RPWD Act 2016 framework. The following key themes emerge: ✓ Disability is a universal human experience affecting approximately 1.3 billion people globally (16% of the world's population). ✓ The WHO's biopsychosocial and human rights-based frameworks have fundamentally transformed how disability is understood and addressed. ✓ India, with one of the world's largest disability populations, has made significant legislative progress through the RPWD Act 2016. ✓ The expansion from 7 to 21 recognized disabilities under RPWD Act 2016 is a major advancement aligned with international standards. ✓ Healthcare, education, and employment disparities for PwDs persist globally and in India, requiring sustained multi-sectoral interventions. ✓ Implementation gaps – financial, institutional, and attitudinal – remain the primary challenge in translating rights into reality. ✓ Data quality and availability on disability in India remain critical barriers to effective policy and program monitoring. 15.2 Identified Research Gaps For thesis purposes, the following research gaps represent significant opportunities for original contribution to the disability studies field in India: → Up-to-date national disability prevalence data (Census 2011 is over a decade old; new census data awaited). → Impact evaluation of RPWD Act 2016 on actual outcomes for PwDs across the 21 disability categories. → Comparative analysis of implementation across different states – gaps and success factors. → Specific outcome data on newly recognized disabilities (SLD, ASD, blood disorders) under RPWD. → Gender-disaggregated analysis of RPWD Act implementation – women with disabilities. → Effectiveness of UDID (Unique Disability ID) system in service delivery.

→ Mental health and psychosocial disability – the most neglected category under RPWD implementation. → Intersectionality of disability with caste, class, and geography in India. → Private sector compliance with RPWD Act provisions and reasons for low adoption. → Community-based rehabilitation effectiveness in rural Indian settings. 15.3 Policy Recommendations  Conduct a dedicated National Disability Survey to update prevalence data (not dependent on census).  Implement RPWD Act Section 48 – mandatory social audit of all disability schemes.  Increase SIPDA budget allocation commensurate with scale of disability burden.  Strengthen National and State Advisory Boards on Disability with time-bound action plans.  Expand rural disability services through integrated community health and rehabilitation workers.  Mandate disability-disaggregated data collection in all government schemes and reports.  Develop awareness campaigns targeting families, communities, employers, and healthcare providers.  Introduce disability-competency training in all healthcare professional curricula.  Enforce accessibility standards through third-party audits and public reporting.  Establish a National Disability Data Observatory for real-time monitoring.

APPENDIX A: CASE STUDIES IN DISABILITY INCLUSION A.1 Case Study: Disability Inclusion in Primary Healthcare – Kerala Kerala is widely regarded as India's best-performing state in disability inclusion, driven by its high literacy, strong public health infrastructure, and progressive welfare policies. The Kerala Social Security Mission (KSSM) and Kerala Viklangam Welfare Corporation have been instrumental in implementing disability rights. Key Features of Kerala's Disability Inclusion Model:  Comprehensive disability database maintained at Panchayat level.  Door-to-door identification and enrollment of PwDs in welfare schemes.  Kanivu – a rehabilitation scheme providing free assistive devices and therapy.  Accessible healthcare facilities across all government hospitals.  Disability pension linked to Aadhaar for direct benefit transfer.  Special schools and rehabilitation centers in every district.  Strong disabled persons' organizations (DPOs) participating in governance. Outcome Indicators for Kerala (vs. national average): Indicator Kerala National Average Disability literacy rate ~72% 54.5% Access to disability pension Relatively comprehensive Under 20% nationally Assistive device access Higher through Kanivu scheme Low (especially rural) School enrollment (disabled children) Higher than national average Significant out-of-school population Healthcare access for PwDs Better infrastructure Significant barriers A.2 Case Study: Autism Spectrum Disorder – Policy and Practice in India ASD was first formally recognized in India through the National Trust Act 1999, which included autism among its four target disabilities alongside cerebral palsy, intellectual disability, and multiple disabilities. Its inclusion in the RPWD Act 2016 further strengthened legal protections. → India has no national epidemiological survey on ASD prevalence. Small-scale studies suggest 1–1.5% prevalence.

→ Services for ASD are concentrated in urban areas and private sector, creating access inequities. → National Trust provides SAMARTH scheme – early intervention and respite care for children with ASD. → Government has established National Institute of Mental Health & Neurosciences (NIMHANS) as a national referral center. → RPWD Act 2016 guarantees education, employment, and social security for persons with ASD. → Research gap: Population-based ASD survey in India urgently needed. → Parent organizations like Action for Autism and AAPC play critical advocacy roles. A.3 Case Study: Thalassemia – India's Blood Disorder Challenge India faces the world's highest burden of thalassemia, making its inclusion in RPWD Act 2016 particularly significant. The human, economic, and social impact of thalassemia in India underscores the need for integrated disability, prevention, and treatment policies.  India has approximately 100,000 children living with thalassemia major.  10,000–15,000 new thalassemia major children are born each year in India.  Approximately 4.4 crore (44 million) Indians carry the thalassemia trait – the highest globally.  Regular blood transfusions (every 2–4 weeks) are required for thalassemia major patients.  Bone marrow transplant is the only cure but is expensive (Rs. 10–15 lakh) and not universally accessible.  Chelation therapy (iron removal) is required alongside transfusions, adding to cost burden.  RPWD Act recognition means thalassemia patients now have access to 4% reservation in government employment.  Thalassemia India and Thalassemia International Federation advocate for universal neonatal screening.

APPENDIX B: KEY GLOBAL CONVENTIONS AND FRAMEWORKS B.1 UN Convention on Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD) The UNCRPD (2006) is the most comprehensive international human rights treaty on disability. It entered into force on May 3, 2008, and has been signed by 164 countries and ratified by 185 parties (as of 2023). India ratified the UNCRPD on October 1, 2007. Key Articles of the UNCRPD Relevant to India's RPWD Act: Article Subject Corresponding RPWD Provision Article 3 General Principles (dignity, non-discrimination, inclusion) Preamble & Sec. 3 Article 5 Equality and Non-discrimination Sec. 3 (Non-discrimination) Article 9 Accessibility Sec. 40–46 (Accessibility) Article 12 Equal recognition before the law Sec. 14 (Legal Capacity) Article 13 Access to justice Sec. 12 (Access to Justice) Article 19 Living independently in the community Sec. 5 (Community Inclusion) Article 24 Education Sec. 16–18 (Inclusive Education) Article 27 Work and employment Sec. 19–24 (Employment) Article 28 Adequate standard of living & social protection Sec. 24–30 (Social Security) Article 33 National implementation and monitoring Sec. 60–73 (Chief Commissioner) B.2 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and Disability The 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development (SDGs) explicitly includes persons with disabilities through the principle of 'leave no one behind.' Disability is mentioned explicitly in 11 of the 17 SDGs. SDG Goal Title Disability Relevance SDG 1 No Poverty PwDs disproportionately in poverty SDG 3 Good Health Health equity for PwDs; rehabilitation SDG 4 Quality Education Inclusive and equitable education SDG 8 Decent Work Employment for PwDs (target 8.5) SDG 10 Reduced Inequalities Non-discrimination (target 10.2) SDG 11 Sustainable Cities Accessible urban environments SDG 16 Peace, Justice, Institutions Access to justice for PwDs SDG 17 Partnerships Disability-inclusive data systems

B.3 Incheon Strategy 2013–2022 The Incheon Strategy 'Make the Right Real' for Persons with Disabilities in Asia and the Pacific (2013–2022) provided a regional disability policy framework for the Asia-Pacific region, including India. It set 10 disability-specific goals with measurable targets.  Goal 1: Reduce poverty and enhance work and employment prospects.  Goal 2: Promote participation in political processes and decision-making.  Goal 3: Enhance access to the physical environment, public transportation, knowledge, and communications.  Goal 4: Strengthen social protection.  Goal 5: Expand early intervention and education.  Goal 6: Ensure gender equality and women's empowerment.  Goal 7: Ensure disability-inclusive disaster risk reduction.  Goal 8: Improve reliability and comparability of disability data.  Goal 9: Accelerate the ratification and implementation of UNCRPD.  Goal 10: Advance regional and international cooperation.

APPENDIX D: GLOSSARY OF KEY TERMS Accessibility: The degree to which an environment, product, or service is available and usable by people with disabilities without barriers. Governed by universal design principles. Adaptive Behavior: Conceptual, social, and practical skills learned and performed by people in everyday life. Used in diagnosing intellectual disability. Assistive Technology (AT): Any device, software, or equipment that helps people with disabilities perform functions that might otherwise be difficult or impossible. Benchmark Disability: Under RPWD Act 2016 – a person with not less than 40% of a specified disability as certified by a medical authority. Community-Based Rehabilitation (CBR): A strategy within community development for rehabilitation, equalization of opportunities, poverty reduction, and social inclusion of people with disabilities. Disability: Under RPWD Act 2016 – a person with long-term physical, mental, intellectual or sensory impairment which, in interaction with barriers, hinders full and effective participation in society equally with others. Disabled Persons Organizations (DPOs): Organizations controlled by persons with disabilities (at least 51% of membership) advocating for disability rights and inclusion. Durable Functional Limitation: A long-term or permanent limitation in physical, mental, or sensory functioning. Equal Opportunity Policy (EOP): A document required from all government establishments under RPWD Act specifying measures for employment of PwDs. Habilitation: Helping a person with disability develop skills and abilities they were never able to perform (vs. rehabilitation which restores abilities). ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health): WHO's universal framework for describing health and disability, using a biopsychosocial approach. Inclusion: The process of improving the terms of participation in society for people who are disadvantaged. In disability – ensuring PwDs are part of mainstream society. Locomotor Disability: Disability resulting from affliction of musculoskeletal or nervous system affecting movement, strength, endurance, mobility, or coordination. Participation Restriction: Problems a person with disability may experience when involved in life situations. Reasonable Accommodation: Necessary and appropriate modifications to ensure persons with disabilities can enjoy equal rights on an equal basis.

Rehabilitation: A set of interventions designed to optimize functioning and reduce disability in individuals with health conditions in interaction with their environment. RPWD Act 2016: Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016 (Act 49 of 2016) – India's landmark disability legislation enacted December 2016, in force April 2017. Specified Disability: One of the 21 disabilities listed in the Schedule to the RPWD Act 2016 that are legally recognized disabilities in India. UDID: Unique Disability Identity Card – a government-issued digital disability certificate in India under the Swavalamban Card scheme. UNCRPD: United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (2006) – the international human rights treaty on disability rights. Universal Design: Design of products, environments, programs, and services usable by all people to the greatest extent possible, without need for adaptation.
